Time Discretization for Parabolic Optimal Control Problems

University of Pisa

Overview¶

The previous lectures introduced the continuous optimality system for time-dependent control problems and the functional-analytic setting needed for parabolic PDEs:

  • Gelfand triples V↪H↪V′V\hookrightarrow H\hookrightarrow V'V↪H↪V′;

  • the energy space

    W(0,T):={y∈L2(0,T;V):yt∈L2(0,T;V′)};W(0,T):=\{y\in L^2(0,T;V): y_t\in L^2(0,T;V')\};W(0,T):={y∈L2(0,T;V):yt​∈L2(0,T;V′)};
    (1)

  • forward state equations and backward adjoint equations;

  • variational inequalities and projection formulas for box constraints.

This lecture addresses the next numerical question:

what is the correct discrete optimality system after time discretization?

We focus on one time-stepping method, implicit Euler, and derive the corresponding discrete optimality system carefully. The goal is not to cover all possible time discretizations, but to understand the structure that any reliable implementation must preserve.

The logical path is:

  1. recall the continuous linear-quadratic parabolic control problem;

  2. discretize the state equation in time with implicit Euler;

  3. define the discrete reduced optimization problem;

  4. derive the discrete adjoint equation from a discrete Lagrangian;

  5. identify the discrete gradient and the first-order optimality system;

  6. compare discretize-then-optimize with optimize-then-discretize;

  7. write the fully discrete finite element matrix form;

  8. add box constraints through a time-discrete variational inequality.

The central message is:

the discrete adjoint is not obtained by informally reversing time; it is the transpose, with respect to the chosen discrete inner products, of the linearized discrete state equation.

This is the principle behind adjoint-consistent time discretizations.

Continuous Problem¶

Let

V↪H↪V′V\hookrightarrow H\hookrightarrow V'V↪H↪V′
(2)

be a Gelfand triple, where VVV and HHH are Hilbert spaces, the embedding V↪HV\hookrightarrow HV↪H is dense and continuous, and HHH is identified with its dual.

For the heat equation with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions one may keep in mind

V=H01(Ω),H=L2(Ω),V′=H−1(Ω).V=H_0^1(\Omega), \qquad H=L^2(\Omega), \qquad V'=H^{-1}(\Omega).V=H01​(Ω),H=L2(Ω),V′=H−1(Ω).
(3)

Let UUU be a Hilbert space for the control values, and assume that

B∈L(U,V′)B\in \mathcal L(U,V')B∈L(U,V′)
(4)

is a bounded control operator. Let

f∈L2(0,T;V′),y0∈H,yd∈L2(0,T;H),f\in L^2(0,T;V'), \qquad y_0\in H, \qquad y_d\in L^2(0,T;H),f∈L2(0,T;V′),y0​∈H,yd​∈L2(0,T;H),
(5)

and let α>0\alpha>0α>0.

We consider the state equation

yt(t)+Ay(t)=Bu(t)+f(t)in V′ for a.e. t∈(0,T),y_t(t)+Ay(t)=Bu(t)+f(t) \qquad\text{in }V'\text{ for a.e. }t\in(0,T),yt​(t)+Ay(t)=Bu(t)+f(t)in V′ for a.e. t∈(0,T),
(6)

with initial condition

y(0)=y0.y(0)=y_0.y(0)=y0​.
(7)

Here A:V→V′A:V\to V'A:V→V′ is induced by a bilinear form

a(⋅,⋅):V×V→R,a(\cdot,\cdot):V\times V\to \mathbb R,a(⋅,⋅):V×V→R,
(8)

that is continuous and coercive:

∣a(v,w)∣≤M∥v∥V∥w∥V,a(v,v)≥γ∥v∥V2∀v,w∈V,|a(v,w)|\le M\|v\|_V\|w\|_V, \qquad a(v,v)\ge \gamma \|v\|_V^2 \qquad \forall v,w\in V,∣a(v,w)∣≤M∥v∥V​∥w∥V​,a(v,v)≥γ∥v∥V2​∀v,w∈V,
(9)

for constants M,γ>0M,\gamma>0M,γ>0.

The weak form of the state equation is:

find y∈W(0,T)y\in W(0,T)y∈W(0,T) such that y(0)=y0y(0)=y_0y(0)=y0​ and

⟨yt(t),v⟩V′,V+a(y(t),v)=⟨Bu(t)+f(t),v⟩V′,V∀v∈V\langle y_t(t),v\rangle_{V',V} +a(y(t),v) = \langle Bu(t)+f(t),v\rangle_{V',V} \qquad \forall v\in V⟨yt​(t),v⟩V′,V​+a(y(t),v)=⟨Bu(t)+f(t),v⟩V′,V​∀v∈V
(10)

for almost every t∈(0,T)t\in(0,T)t∈(0,T).

The cost functional is

J(y,u)=12∫0T∥y(t)−yd(t)∥H2 dt+α2∫0T∥u(t)∥U2 dt+β2∥y(T)−yT∥H2.J(y,u) = \frac12\int_0^T \|y(t)-y_d(t)\|_H^2\,dt +\frac\alpha2\int_0^T \|u(t)\|_U^2\,dt + \frac\beta2 \| y(T) - y_T\|_H^2.J(y,u)=21​∫0T​∥y(t)−yd​(t)∥H2​dt+2α​∫0T​∥u(t)∥U2​dt+2β​∥y(T)−yT​∥H2​.
(11)

The unconstrained optimal control problem is

min⁡u∈L2(0,T;U)j(u):=J(y(u),u),\min_{u\in L^2(0,T;U)} j(u):=J(y(u),u),u∈L2(0,T;U)min​j(u):=J(y(u),u),
(12)

where y(u)y(u)y(u) denotes the state associated with uuu.

For constrained problems we replace the full control space by a nonempty closed convex set

Uad⊂L2(0,T;U).U_{\mathrm{ad}}\subset L^2(0,T;U).Uad​⊂L2(0,T;U).
(13)

Continuous Optimality System¶

The continuous first-order system has already been derived in the previous lecture. We recall it only to fix the notation.

For a given control uuu, the state y=y(u)y=y(u)y=y(u) solves

{yt+Ay=Bu+f,y(0)=y0.\begin{cases} y_t+Ay=Bu+f,\\ y(0)=y_0. \end{cases}{yt​+Ay=Bu+f,y(0)=y0​.​
(14)

The corresponding adjoint ppp solves the backward parabolic problem

{−pt+A∗p=M(y−yd),p(T)=β(y(T)−yT).\begin{cases} -p_t+A^*p=M(y-y_d),\\ p(T)=\beta(y(T)-y_T). \end{cases}{−pt​+A∗p=M(y−yd​),p(T)=β(y(T)−yT​).​
(15)

In weak form this means

−⟨pt(t),v⟩V′,V+a(v,p(t))=(y(t)−yd(t),v)H∀v∈V.-\langle p_t(t),v\rangle_{V',V} +a(v,p(t)) = (y(t)-y_d(t),v)_H \qquad \forall v\in V.−⟨pt​(t),v⟩V′,V​+a(v,p(t))=(y(t)−yd​(t),v)H​∀v∈V.
(16)

Here A∗A^*A∗ is the adjoint operator. If aaa is symmetric, then A∗=AA^*=AA∗=A.

The reduced gradient is

∇j(u)=αu+B∗pin L2(0,T;U),\nabla j(u)=\alpha u+B^*p \qquad\text{in }L^2(0,T;U),∇j(u)=αu+B∗pin L2(0,T;U),
(17)

with the usual interpretation of B∗B^*B∗ through duality:

(B∗p,w)U=⟨Bw,p⟩V′,V.(B^*p,w)_U = \langle Bw,p\rangle_{V',V}.(B∗p,w)U​=⟨Bw,p⟩V′,V​.
(18)

Thus, in the unconstrained case, the optimality system is

{yˉt+Ayˉ=Buˉ+f,yˉ(0)=y0,−pˉt+A∗pˉ=M(yˉ−yd),pˉ(T)=β(y(T)−yT),αuˉ+B∗pˉ=0.\begin{cases} \bar y_t+A\bar y=B\bar u+f,\\ \bar y(0)=y_0,\\ -\bar p_t+A^*\bar p=M(\bar y-y_d),\\ \bar p(T)=\beta(y(T)-y_T),\\ \alpha \bar u+B^*\bar p=0. \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​yˉ​t​+Ayˉ​=Buˉ+f,yˉ​(0)=y0​,−pˉ​t​+A∗pˉ​=M(yˉ​−yd​),pˉ​(T)=β(y(T)−yT​),αuˉ+B∗pˉ​=0.​
(19)

For a closed convex admissible set UadU_{\mathrm{ad}}Uad​, the last equation is replaced by the variational inequality

∫0T(αuˉ(t)+B∗pˉ(t),u(t)−uˉ(t))U dt≥0∀u∈Uad.\int_0^T (\alpha \bar u(t)+B^*\bar p(t), u(t)-\bar u(t))_U\,dt \ge 0 \qquad \forall u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}.∫0T​(αuˉ(t)+B∗pˉ​(t),u(t)−uˉ(t))U​dt≥0∀u∈Uad​.
(20)

The purpose of this lecture is to build the discrete analogue of this system from the discrete optimization problem itself.

Implicit Euler Discretization of the State¶

Let

0=t0<t1<⋯<tN=T,τ:=tk−tk−1=TN.0=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_N=T, \qquad \tau:=t_k-t_{k-1}=\frac{T}{N}.0=t0​<t1​<⋯<tN​=T,τ:=tk​−tk−1​=NT​.
(21)

We use one control value uk∈Uu^k\in Uuk∈U on each interval

Ik:=(tk−1,tk],k=1,…,N.I_k:=(t_{k-1},t_k], \qquad k=1,\ldots,N.Ik​:=(tk−1​,tk​],k=1,…,N.
(22)

The data are approximated by values

fk∈V′,ydk∈H.f^k\in V', \qquad y_d^k\in H.fk∈V′,ydk​∈H.
(23)

For instance, one may take time averages

fk:=1τ∫tk−1tkf(t) dt,ydk:=1τ∫tk−1tkyd(t) dt.f^k:=\frac1\tau\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_k} f(t)\,dt, \qquad y_d^k:=\frac1\tau\int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_k} y_d(t)\,dt.fk:=τ1​∫tk−1​tk​​f(t)dt,ydk​:=τ1​∫tk−1​tk​​yd​(t)dt.
(24)

The implicit Euler approximation of the state equation is:

find y1,…,yN∈Vy^1,\ldots,y^N\in Vy1,…,yN∈V, with y0=y0y^0=y_0y0=y0​, such that

(yk−yk−1τ,v)H+a(yk,v)=⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V∀v∈V,\left(\frac{y^k-y^{k-1}}{\tau},v\right)_H +a(y^k,v) = \langle Bu^k+f^k,v\rangle_{V',V} \qquad \forall v\in V,(τyk−yk−1​,v)H​+a(yk,v)=⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V​∀v∈V,
(25)

for k=1,…,Nk=1,\ldots,Nk=1,…,N.

Equivalently,

(yk,v)H+τa(yk,v)=(yk−1,v)H+τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V.(y^k,v)_H+\tau a(y^k,v) = (y^{k-1},v)_H +\tau\langle Bu^k+f^k,v\rangle_{V',V}.(yk,v)H​+τa(yk,v)=(yk−1,v)H​+τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V​.
(26)

This is a forward time-stepping scheme. Once yk−1y^{k-1}yk−1 and uku^kuk are known, the equation for yky^kyk is an elliptic problem with bilinear form

(y,v)H+τa(y,v).(y,v)_H+\tau a(y,v).(y,v)H​+τa(y,v).
(27)

Coercivity follows immediately:

(v,v)H+τa(v,v)≥∥v∥H2+τγ∥v∥V2.(v,v)_H+\tau a(v,v) \ge \|v\|_H^2+\tau\gamma\|v\|_V^2.(v,v)H​+τa(v,v)≥∥v∥H2​+τγ∥v∥V2​.
(28)

Therefore each time step is well posed by the Lax-Milgram theorem.

Proposition. For every sequence of controls

uτ=(u1,…,uN)∈UNu_\tau=(u^1,\ldots,u^N)\in U^Nuτ​=(u1,…,uN)∈UN
(29)

there exists a unique discrete state sequence

yτ=(y1,…,yN)∈VN.y_\tau=(y^1,\ldots,y^N)\in V^N.yτ​=(y1,…,yN)∈VN.
(30)

Moreover, the map

Sτ:UN→VN,uτ↦yτ,S_\tau:U^N\to V^N, \qquad u_\tau\mapsto y_\tau,Sτ​:UN→VN,uτ​↦yτ​,
(31)

is affine and continuous.

Proof. For fixed kkk, assume yk−1y^{k-1}yk−1 is already known. The left-hand side is the coercive bilinear form

mτ(y,v):=(y,v)H+τa(y,v),m_\tau(y,v):=(y,v)_H+\tau a(y,v),mτ​(y,v):=(y,v)H​+τa(y,v),
(32)

and the right-hand side

ℓk(v):=(yk−1,v)H+τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V\ell_k(v):=(y^{k-1},v)_H +\tau\langle Bu^k+f^k,v\rangle_{V',V}ℓk​(v):=(yk−1,v)H​+τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V​
(33)

is a bounded linear functional on VVV. Hence yky^kyk exists and is unique. Induction over k=1,…,Nk=1,\ldots,Nk=1,…,N gives the full discrete state.

Linearity in the variables u1,…,uNu^1,\ldots,u^Nu1,…,uN and affine dependence on (y0,f1,…,fN)(y_0,f^1,\ldots,f^N)(y0​,f1,…,fN) follow directly from the recursion. Continuity follows from the stability estimate obtained by applying Lax-Milgram at each step and iterating over finitely many time levels. □\square□

The Discrete Optimization Problem¶

The implicit Euler state equation defines a finite-dimensional-in-time optimization problem.

The discrete cost functional is

Jτ(yτ,uτ):=τ2∑k=1N∥yk−ydk∥H2+ατ2∑k=1N∥uk∥U2.J_\tau(y_\tau,u_\tau) := \frac{\tau}{2}\sum_{k=1}^N \|y^k-y_d^k\|_H^2 +\frac{\alpha\tau}{2}\sum_{k=1}^N \|u^k\|_U^2.Jτ​(yτ​,uτ​):=2τ​k=1∑N​∥yk−ydk​∥H2​+2ατ​k=1∑N​∥uk∥U2​.
(34)

The reduced discrete functional is

jτ(uτ):=Jτ(Sτuτ,uτ).j_\tau(u_\tau):=J_\tau(S_\tau u_\tau,u_\tau).jτ​(uτ​):=Jτ​(Sτ​uτ​,uτ​).
(35)

The unconstrained discrete problem is

min⁡uτ∈UNjτ(uτ).\min_{u_\tau\in U^N} j_\tau(u_\tau).uτ​∈UNmin​jτ​(uτ​).
(36)

For constrained controls, a natural time-discrete admissible set is

Uad,τ⊂UN.U_{\mathrm{ad},\tau} \subset U^N.Uad,τ​⊂UN.
(37)

For box constraints this will be specified later level by level.

Since the state equation is linear and the cost functional is quadratic, the reduced functional jτj_\taujτ​ is quadratic. Because α>0\alpha>0α>0, it is strictly convex in the control. Therefore the unconstrained problem has a unique minimizer, and the constrained problem has a unique minimizer whenever Uad,τU_{\mathrm{ad},\tau}Uad,τ​ is nonempty, closed, and convex.

The remaining task is to compute the gradient of jτj_\taujτ​ without explicitly forming the derivative of SτS_\tauSτ​.

Discrete Lagrangian¶

We now derive the discrete adjoint equation directly from the time-discrete optimization problem. This is the discretize-then-optimize approach.

For each time level define the state residual

Rk(yτ,uτ)∈V′R^k(y_\tau,u_\tau)\in V'Rk(yτ​,uτ​)∈V′
(38)

by

⟨Rk(yτ,uτ),v⟩V′,V:=(yk−yk−1,v)H+τa(yk,v)−τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V.\langle R^k(y_\tau,u_\tau),v\rangle_{V',V} := (y^k-y^{k-1},v)_H +\tau a(y^k,v) -\tau\langle Bu^k+f^k,v\rangle_{V',V}.⟨Rk(yτ​,uτ​),v⟩V′,V​:=(yk−yk−1,v)H​+τa(yk,v)−τ⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V​.
(39)

The discrete state equation is

Rk(yτ,uτ)=0,k=1,…,N.R^k(y_\tau,u_\tau)=0, \qquad k=1,\ldots,N.Rk(yτ​,uτ​)=0,k=1,…,N.
(40)

Introduce Lagrange multipliers

p1,…,pN∈V.p^1,\ldots,p^N\in V.p1,…,pN∈V.
(41)

We choose the discrete Lagrangian

Lτ(yτ,uτ,pτ):=Jτ(yτ,uτ)−∑k=1N⟨Rk(yτ,uτ),pk⟩V′,V.\mathscr L_\tau(y_\tau,u_\tau,p_\tau) := J_\tau(y_\tau,u_\tau) -\sum_{k=1}^N \langle R^k(y_\tau,u_\tau),p^k\rangle_{V',V}.Lτ​(yτ​,uτ​,pτ​):=Jτ​(yτ​,uτ​)−k=1∑N​⟨Rk(yτ​,uτ​),pk⟩V′,V​.
(42)

That is,

Lτ=τ2∑k=1N∥yk−ydk∥H2+ατ2∑k=1N∥uk∥U2−∑k=1N[(yk−yk−1,pk)H+τa(yk,pk)−τ⟨Buk+fk,pk⟩V′,V].\begin{aligned} \mathscr L_\tau =& \frac{\tau}{2}\sum_{k=1}^N \|y^k-y_d^k\|_H^2 +\frac{\alpha\tau}{2}\sum_{k=1}^N \|u^k\|_U^2 \\ &- \sum_{k=1}^N \Big[ (y^k-y^{k-1},p^k)_H +\tau a(y^k,p^k) -\tau\langle Bu^k+f^k,p^k\rangle_{V',V} \Big]. \end{aligned}Lτ​=​2τ​k=1∑N​∥yk−ydk​∥H2​+2ατ​k=1∑N​∥uk∥U2​−k=1∑N​[(yk−yk−1,pk)H​+τa(yk,pk)−τ⟨Buk+fk,pk⟩V′,V​].​
(43)

This sign convention is chosen so that the discrete adjoint equation has the same sign as the continuous adjoint equation and the reduced gradient is αuk+B∗pk\alpha u^k+B^*p^kαuk+B∗pk.

Variation with Respect to the State¶

Let z1,…,zN∈Vz^1,\ldots,z^N\in Vz1,…,zN∈V be arbitrary variations of the state. The initial value y0=y0y^0=y_0y0=y0​ is fixed, so z0=0z^0=0z0=0.

The derivative of the tracking term is

τ∑k=1N(yk−ydk,zk)H.\tau\sum_{k=1}^N (y^k-y_d^k,z^k)_H.τk=1∑N​(yk−ydk​,zk)H​.
(44)

The derivative of the residual part in the Lagrangian is

−∑k=1N[(zk−zk−1,pk)H+τa(zk,pk)].- \sum_{k=1}^N \Big[ (z^k-z^{k-1},p^k)_H +\tau a(z^k,p^k) \Big].−k=1∑N​[(zk−zk−1,pk)H​+τa(zk,pk)].
(45)

The term containing zkz^kzk appears in two neighboring residuals, with the overall minus sign from the Lagrangian:

  • from RkR^kRk, through (zk,pk)H+τa(zk,pk)(z^k,p^k)_H+\tau a(z^k,p^k)(zk,pk)H​+τa(zk,pk);

  • from Rk+1R^{k+1}Rk+1, through −(zk,pk+1)H-(z^k,p^{k+1})_H−(zk,pk+1)H​.

For k=1,…,N−1k=1,\ldots,N-1k=1,…,N−1, the coefficient of zkz^kzk is therefore

τ(yk−ydk,zk)H−(zk,pk)H+(zk,pk+1)H−τa(zk,pk).\tau(y^k-y_d^k,z^k)_H -(z^k,p^k)_H +(z^k,p^{k+1})_H -\tau a(z^k,p^k).τ(yk−ydk​,zk)H​−(zk,pk)H​+(zk,pk+1)H​−τa(zk,pk).
(46)

For k=Nk=Nk=N, there is no residual RN+1R^{N+1}RN+1. It is convenient to introduce the terminal value

pN+1:=β(yN−yT).p^{N+1}:=\beta(y^N-y_T).pN+1:=β(yN−yT​).
(47)

Then all time levels can be written uniformly as

τ(yk−ydk,zk)H−(zk,pk−pk+1)H−τa(zk,pk).\tau(y^k-y_d^k,z^k)_H -(z^k,p^k-p^{k+1})_H -\tau a(z^k,p^k).τ(yk−ydk​,zk)H​−(zk,pk−pk+1)H​−τa(zk,pk).
(48)

The stationarity condition with respect to yky^kyk is therefore:

find p1,…,pN∈Vp^1,\ldots,p^N\in Vp1,…,pN∈V, with pN+1=β(yN−yT)p^{N+1}=\beta(y^N-y_T)pN+1=β(yN−yT​), such that

(z,pk−pk+1)H+τa(z,pk)=τ(yk−ydk,z)H∀z∈V,(z,p^k-p^{k+1})_H +\tau a(z,p^k) = \tau (y^k-y_d^k,z)_H \qquad \forall z\in V,(z,pk−pk+1)H​+τa(z,pk)=τ(yk−ydk​,z)H​∀z∈V,
(49)

for k=N,N−1,…,1k=N,N-1,\ldots,1k=N,N−1,…,1.

Dividing by τ\tauτ gives

(pk−pk+1τ,z)H+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk,z)H.\left(\frac{p^k-p^{k+1}}{\tau},z\right)_H +a(z,p^k) = (y^k-y_d^k,z)_H.(τpk−pk+1​,z)H​+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk​,z)H​.
(50)

This is an implicit Euler scheme run backward in time for the adjoint equation

−pt+A∗p=y−yd,p(T)=β(y(T)−yT).-p_t+A^*p=y-y_d, \qquad p(T)=\beta(y(T)-y_T).−pt​+A∗p=y−yd​,p(T)=β(y(T)−yT​).
(51)

In boxed form:

(pk−pk+1τ,z)H+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk,z)H∀z∈V,pN+1=β(yN−yT).\boxed{ \left(\frac{p^k-p^{k+1}}{\tau},z\right)_H +a(z,p^k) = (y^k-y_d^k,z)_H \quad \forall z\in V, \qquad p^{N+1}=\beta(y^N-y_T). }(τpk−pk+1​,z)H​+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk​,z)H​∀z∈V,pN+1=β(yN−yT​).​
(52)

This equation is solved backward:

k=N,N−1,…,1.k=N,N-1,\ldots,1.k=N,N−1,…,1.
(53)

Variation with Respect to the Control¶

We now compute the derivative with respect to uku^kuk.

Using the same Lagrangian convention as above, the control derivative at time level kkk is

τα(uk,w)U+τ⟨Bw,pk⟩V′,V\tau\alpha(u^k,w)_U +\tau\langle Bw,p^k\rangle_{V',V}τα(uk,w)U​+τ⟨Bw,pk⟩V′,V​
(54)

for every control variation w∈Uw\in Uw∈U.

By definition of B∗B^*B∗,

⟨Bw,pk⟩V′,V=(B∗pk,w)U.\langle Bw,p^k\rangle_{V',V} = (B^*p^k,w)_U.⟨Bw,pk⟩V′,V​=(B∗pk,w)U​.
(55)

Hence

∂jτ∂uk(uτ)=τ(αuk+B∗pk)\frac{\partial j_\tau}{\partial u^k}(u_\tau) = \tau(\alpha u^k+B^*p^k)∂uk∂jτ​​(uτ​)=τ(αuk+B∗pk)
(56)

as an element of UUU when the product space UNU^NUN is equipped with the unweighted product inner product.

It is often more natural to equip the discrete control space with the time-weighted inner product

(uτ,wτ)Uτ:=τ∑k=1N(uk,wk)U.(u_\tau,w_\tau)_{U_\tau} := \tau\sum_{k=1}^N (u^k,w^k)_U.(uτ​,wτ​)Uτ​​:=τk=1∑N​(uk,wk)U​.
(57)

With respect to this inner product, the gradient is

∇jτ(uτ)k=αuk+B∗pk,k=1,…,N.\nabla j_\tau(u_\tau)^k = \alpha u^k+B^*p^k, \qquad k=1,\ldots,N.∇jτ​(uτ​)k=αuk+B∗pk,k=1,…,N.
(58)

This distinction is important in implementations:

  • the algebraic derivative contains the quadrature weight τ\tauτ;

  • the L2(0,T;U)L^2(0,T;U)L2(0,T;U)-consistent gradient does not.

Discrete Optimality System¶

The unconstrained discrete optimality system is:

find

(y1,…,yN),(p1,…,pN),(u1,…,uN),(y^1,\ldots,y^N), \qquad (p^1,\ldots,p^N), \qquad (u^1,\ldots,u^N),(y1,…,yN),(p1,…,pN),(u1,…,uN),
(59)

with y0=y0y^0=y_0y0=y0​ and pN+1=β(yN−yT)p^{N+1}=\beta(y^N-y_T)pN+1=β(yN−yT​), such that for all v,z∈Vv,z\in Vv,z∈V and w∈Uw\in Uw∈U:

(yk−yk−1τ,v)H+a(yk,v)=⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V\boxed{ \left(\frac{y^k-y^{k-1}}{\tau},v\right)_H +a(y^k,v) = \langle Bu^k+f^k,v\rangle_{V',V} }(τyk−yk−1​,v)H​+a(yk,v)=⟨Buk+fk,v⟩V′,V​​
(60)

for k=1,…,Nk=1,\ldots,Nk=1,…,N,

(pk−pk+1τ,z)H+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk,z)H\boxed{ \left(\frac{p^k-p^{k+1}}{\tau},z\right)_H +a(z,p^k) = (y^k-y_d^k,z)_H }(τpk−pk+1​,z)H​+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk​,z)H​​
(61)

for k=N,…,1k=N,\ldots,1k=N,…,1, and

αuk+B∗pk=0\boxed{ \alpha u^k+B^*p^k=0 }αuk+B∗pk=0​
(62)

for k=1,…,Nk=1,\ldots,Nk=1,…,N.

The structure is forward-backward:

  • the state equation propagates y0↦y1↦⋯↦yNy^0\mapsto y^1\mapsto\cdots\mapsto y^Ny0↦y1↦⋯↦yN;

  • the adjoint equation propagates pN+1↦pN↦⋯↦p1p^{N+1}\mapsto p^N\mapsto\cdots\mapsto p^1pN+1↦pN↦⋯↦p1;

  • the control equation couples state and adjoint at the same time level.

This is the time-discrete analogue of the continuous parabolic optimality system.

Discretize-Then-Optimize and Optimize-Then-Discretize¶

There are two common routes to a numerical optimality system.

Discretize then optimize (DTO). First discretize the state equation and the objective functional. This gives a finite-dimensional or finite-time-dimensional optimization problem. Then derive its first-order optimality conditions.

This is what we did above.

Optimize then discretize (OTD). First derive the continuous optimality system:

{yt+Ay=Bu+f,−pt+A∗p=y−yd,αu+B∗p=0.\begin{cases} y_t+Ay=Bu+f,\\ -p_t+A^*p=y-y_d,\\ \alpha u+B^*p=0. \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​yt​+Ay=Bu+f,−pt​+A∗p=y−yd​,αu+B∗p=0.​
(63)

Then discretize this coupled forward-backward system.

For implicit Euler, the DTO adjoint equation is

(pk−pk+1τ,z)H+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk,z)H.\left(\frac{p^k-p^{k+1}}{\tau},z\right)_H +a(z,p^k) = (y^k-y_d^k,z)_H.(τpk−pk+1​,z)H​+a(z,pk)=(yk−ydk​,z)H​.
(64)

This is a backward implicit Euler discretization of the adjoint equation. Thus, for this simple linear-quadratic problem, DTO and OTD can be made to match if the adjoint equation is discretized with the time-reversed scheme that is algebraically adjoint to the state scheme.

The important warning is that this agreement is not automatic. It depends on:

  • the quadrature rule used in the objective;

  • the time locations of uku^kuk, yky^kyk, and pkp^kpk;

  • the discrete inner products used to identify gradients;

  • the treatment of terminal terms;

  • the choice of time-stepping method.

DTO guarantees that the computed adjoint is the exact adjoint of the discrete state equation. OTD gives the same result only if the discretization is chosen in an adjoint-consistent way.

For gradient-based optimization of a discretized problem, DTO is usually the safer point of view: the gradient being used is genuinely the gradient of the discrete objective.

Fully Discrete Finite Element Form¶

We now add a standard finite element discretization in space.

Let

Vh⊂VV_h\subset VVh​⊂V
(65)

be a finite element space with basis {φi}i=1ny\{\varphi_i\}_{i=1}^{n_y}{φi​}i=1ny​​. Let the control be represented in a finite-dimensional space UhU_hUh​ with basis {ψj}j=1nu\{\psi_j\}_{j=1}^{n_u}{ψj​}j=1nu​​.

Write the coefficient vectors as

Yk∈Rny,Pk∈Rny,Uk∈Rnu.Y^k\in\mathbb R^{n_y}, \qquad P^k\in\mathbb R^{n_y}, \qquad U^k\in\mathbb R^{n_u}.Yk∈Rny​,Pk∈Rny​,Uk∈Rnu​.
(66)

Define the mass and stiffness matrices

Mij:=(φj,φi)H,Kij:=a(φj,φi).M_{ij}:=(\varphi_j,\varphi_i)_H, \qquad K_{ij}:=a(\varphi_j,\varphi_i).Mij​:=(φj​,φi​)H​,Kij​:=a(φj​,φi​).
(67)

Define also the discrete control operator BhB_hBh​ by

(Bh)ij:=⟨Bψj,φi⟩V′,V.(B_h)_{ij}:=\langle B\psi_j,\varphi_i\rangle_{V',V}.(Bh​)ij​:=⟨Bψj​,φi​⟩V′,V​.
(68)

The state equation becomes

(M+τK)Yk=MYk−1+τBhUk+τFk.\boxed{ (M+\tau K)Y^k = MY^{k-1} +\tau B_h U^k +\tau F^k. }(M+τK)Yk=MYk−1+τBh​Uk+τFk.​
(69)

The adjoint equation becomes

(M+τK)TPk=MTPk+1+τM(Yk−Ydk).\boxed{ (M+\tau K)^T P^k = M^T P^{k+1} +\tau M(Y^k-Y_d^k). }(M+τK)TPk=MTPk+1+τM(Yk−Ydk​).​
(70)

If MMM and KKK are symmetric, this simplifies to

(M+τK)Pk=MPk+1+τM(Yk−Ydk).(M+\tau K)P^k = MP^{k+1} +\tau M(Y^k-Y_d^k).(M+τK)Pk=MPk+1+τM(Yk−Ydk​).
(71)

Let MuM_uMu​ be the control mass matrix. The discrete control equation is

αMuUk+BhTPk=0.\boxed{ \alpha M_u U^k + B_h^T P^k = 0. }αMu​Uk+BhT​Pk=0.​
(72)

Equivalently, after applying Mu−1M_u^{-1}Mu−1​,

αUk+Mu−1BhTPk=0.\alpha U^k + M_u^{-1}B_h^T P^k=0.αUk+Mu−1​BhT​Pk=0.
(73)

This last form is the coefficient representation of the L2L^2L2-gradient in the control space. As in the elliptic case, one must distinguish:

  • the algebraic residual αMuUk+BhTPk\alpha M_u U^k+B_h^T P^kαMu​Uk+BhT​Pk;

  • the Riesz-represented gradient αUk+Mu−1BhTPk\alpha U^k+M_u^{-1}B_h^T P^kαUk+Mu−1​BhT​Pk.

This distinction becomes important for projected-gradient and active-set methods.

All-at-Once Structure¶

The time-stepping form suggests solving the state forward and the adjoint backward. However, the whole system can also be assembled as one global space-time KKT system.

Define

Aτ:=M+τK.A_\tau:=M+\tau K.Aτ​:=M+τK.
(74)

The state equations are

AτY1−τBhU1=MY0+τF1,A_\tau Y^1-\tau B_hU^1 = MY^0+\tau F^1,Aτ​Y1−τBh​U1=MY0+τF1,
(75)

and, for k=2,…,Nk=2,\ldots,Nk=2,…,N,

−MYk−1+AτYk−τBhUk=τFk.-MY^{k-1}+A_\tau Y^k-\tau B_hU^k=\tau F^k.−MYk−1+Aτ​Yk−τBh​Uk=τFk.
(76)

Thus the global state operator is block lower bidiagonal:

E=(Aτ−MAτ−MAτ⋱⋱−MAτ).E = \begin{pmatrix} A_\tau \\ -M & A_\tau \\ & -M & A_\tau \\ & & \ddots & \ddots \\ & & & -M & A_\tau \end{pmatrix}.E=⎝⎛​Aτ​−M​Aτ​−M​Aτ​⋱​⋱−M​Aτ​​⎠⎞​.
(77)

The adjoint operator is the transpose block structure:

ET=(AτT−MTAτT−MT⋱⋱AτT−MTAτT).E^T = \begin{pmatrix} A_\tau^T & -M^T \\ & A_\tau^T & -M^T \\ & & \ddots & \ddots \\ & & & A_\tau^T & -M^T \\ & & & & A_\tau^T \end{pmatrix}.ET=⎝⎛​AτT​​−MTAτT​​−MT⋱​⋱AτT​​−MTAτT​​⎠⎞​.
(78)

This is the algebraic reason why the adjoint runs backward in time.

The full unconstrained KKT system has the schematic form

(τMyET0E0−τB0−τBTατMu)(YPU)=(τMyYdFτ,y00).\begin{pmatrix} \tau \mathcal M_y & E^T & 0\\ E & 0 & -\tau\mathcal B\\ 0 & -\tau\mathcal B^T & \alpha\tau\mathcal M_u \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} Y\\ P\\ U \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \tau\mathcal M_yY_d\\ F_{\tau,y_0}\\ 0 \end{pmatrix}.⎝⎛​τMy​E0​ET0−τBT​0−τBατMu​​⎠⎞​⎝⎛​YPU​⎠⎞​=⎝⎛​τMy​Yd​Fτ,y0​​0​⎠⎞​.
(79)

Here My\mathcal M_yMy​, Mu\mathcal M_uMu​, and B\mathcal BB are block-diagonal space-time matrices containing MMM, MuM_uMu​, and BhB_hBh​ at each time level.

This all-at-once formulation is useful because it exposes the saddle-point structure of the full problem. It is also the natural form for space-time preconditioners. In this lecture, however, the main point is more basic:

the backward adjoint equation is the transpose of the forward time-stepping operator.

Box Constraints¶

Let the continuous admissible set be a pointwise box in time:

Uad={u∈L2(0,T;U):ua(t)≤u(t)≤ub(t) for a.e. t∈(0,T)}.U_{\mathrm{ad}} = \{u\in L^2(0,T;U): u_a(t)\le u(t)\le u_b(t) \text{ for a.e. }t\in(0,T)\}.Uad​={u∈L2(0,T;U):ua​(t)≤u(t)≤ub​(t) for a.e. t∈(0,T)}.
(80)

After time discretization we obtain bounds

uak≤uk≤ubk,k=1,…,N,u_a^k\le u^k\le u_b^k, \qquad k=1,\ldots,N,uak​≤uk≤ubk​,k=1,…,N,
(81)

and the discrete admissible set

Uad,τ={(u1,…,uN)∈UN:uak≤uk≤ubk, k=1,…,N}.U_{\mathrm{ad},\tau} = \{(u^1,\ldots,u^N)\in U^N: u_a^k\le u^k\le u_b^k,\ k=1,\ldots,N\}.Uad,τ​={(u1,…,uN)∈UN:uak​≤uk≤ubk​, k=1,…,N}.
(82)

The discrete first-order condition is the variational inequality

τ∑k=1N(αuˉk+B∗pˉk,uk−uˉk)U≥0∀uτ∈Uad,τ.\tau\sum_{k=1}^N (\alpha \bar u^k+B^*\bar p^k, u^k-\bar u^k)_U \ge 0 \qquad \forall u_\tau\in U_{\mathrm{ad},\tau}.τk=1∑N​(αuˉk+B∗pˉ​k,uk−uˉk)U​≥0∀uτ​∈Uad,τ​.
(83)

Since τ>0\tau>0τ>0, this is equivalent to

∑k=1N(αuˉk+B∗pˉk,uk−uˉk)U≥0∀uτ∈Uad,τ.\sum_{k=1}^N (\alpha \bar u^k+B^*\bar p^k, u^k-\bar u^k)_U \ge 0 \qquad \forall u_\tau\in U_{\mathrm{ad},\tau}.k=1∑N​(αuˉk+B∗pˉ​k,uk−uˉk)U​≥0∀uτ​∈Uad,τ​.
(84)

For a box constraint, the projection formula is level-wise:

uˉk=P[uak,ubk](−1αB∗pˉk),k=1,…,N.\boxed{ \bar u^k = P_{[u_a^k,u_b^k]} \left( -\frac1\alpha B^*\bar p^k \right), \qquad k=1,\ldots,N. }uˉk=P[uak​,ubk​]​(−α1​B∗pˉ​k),k=1,…,N.​
(85)

In coefficient form, if UkU^kUk denotes the vector of control coefficients, the Riesz-represented gradient is

Guk=αUk+Mu−1BhTPk.G_u^k = \alpha U^k+M_u^{-1}B_h^T P^k.Guk​=αUk+Mu−1​BhT​Pk.
(86)

This is the quantity that should be compared with primal bound gaps in projected-gradient or active-set methods. The raw algebraic residual

αMuUk+BhTPk\alpha M_uU^k+B_h^TP^kαMu​Uk+BhT​Pk
(87)

lives in the dual coefficient space and has different scaling.

Thus the active-set logic from the elliptic case applies independently at each time level, but the state and adjoint equations still couple all time levels through forward and backward propagation.

Reduced Numerical Strategy¶

The reduced viewpoint eliminates the state and adjoint from the optimization variables.

Given a control sequence uτu_\tauuτ​:

  1. solve the state equation forward for y1,…,yNy^1,\ldots,y^Ny1,…,yN;

  2. solve the adjoint equation backward for pN,…,p1p^N,\ldots,p^1pN,…,p1;

  3. assemble the gradient

    gk=αuk+B∗pk;g^k=\alpha u^k+B^*p^k;gk=αuk+B∗pk;
    (88)

  4. update the control with a gradient, conjugate-gradient, quasi-Newton, or projected method.

The cost of one gradient evaluation is essentially:

  • one forward parabolic solve;

  • one backward parabolic solve;

  • one application of B∗B^*B∗ at each time level.

This is why the adjoint method is indispensable: the cost of the gradient does not scale with the number of control degrees of freedom.

For box-constrained problems the same loop is used, but the update step is projected or active-set based.

What Should Be Remembered¶

Time discretization is not just a technical detail. It determines the exact algebraic optimization problem being solved.

For implicit Euler:

  • the state equation is a forward recurrence;

  • the discrete adjoint is a backward recurrence;

  • the adjoint recurrence is the transpose of the state recurrence;

  • the discrete gradient at time level kkk is

    αuk+B∗pk;\alpha u^k+B^*p^k;αuk+B∗pk;
    (89)

  • in finite elements the control gradient requires the control mass matrix;

  • box constraints give a projection formula at each time level.

The key DTO lesson is:

derive the adjoint from the discrete equations if you want the exact gradient of the discrete objective.

The OTD viewpoint is still valuable, because it explains what the discrete system approximates. But for implementation and optimization, DTO is the most reliable way to avoid sign errors, index shifts, and inconsistent gradients.

References¶

  • F. Troeltzsch, Optimal Control of Partial Differential Equations, AMS, 2010.

  • J. C. De los Reyes, Numerical PDE-Constrained Optimization, Springer, 2015.

  • M. Hinze, R. Pinnau, M. Ulbrich, and S. Ulbrich, Optimization with PDE Constraints, Springer, 2009.

  • A. Manzoni, A. Quarteroni, S. Salsa, Optimal Control of Partial Differential Equations, Springer, 2021.
